summary_text:
Chief Complaint
Left hand pain and difficulty controlling alcohol consumption.

History of Present Illness
The patient reports experiencing pain in his left hand for several weeks, exacerbated by activity. He describes the pain as sharp and intermittent. The patient also reports struggling with not drinking and experiencing urges to drink. He reports experiencing headaches and tiredness after starting lisinopril. He reports experiencing swooshing sounds in his left ear after the first time taking lisinopril. The patient reports feeling lethargic and tired after the first time taking lisinopril. He denies chest tightness or pressure. The patient reports experiencing nausea.

Findings
The patient's morning blood pressure readings are between 130 and 145 or 150 mmHg, and the evening readings are between 95 and 100 mmHg. The patient reports blood pressure readings of 150 mmHg in the evening. The patient reports blood pressure readings between 95 and 100 mmHg in the morning. The patient reports experiencing dizziness. The patient reports experiencing anxiety. The patient reports experiencing tiredness. The patient reports experiencing swooshing sounds in their left ear after the first time taking lisinopril.

Assessment
The patient is experiencing left hand pain and is struggling with alcohol urges. The doctor's impression is alcohol use disorder.

Plan
The doctor will administer a shot to the patient. The doctor recommends upgrading influenza vaccination. The doctor recommends shingrix. The doctor plans to discuss medicine to help with alcohol urges. Vivitrol may be administered as an alternative to naltrexone. Naltrexone will be started on May 17th. The patient will have a follow-up in June. Lisinopril will be started. Hydrochlorothiazide will be started.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Hypertension.
Alcohol use disorder.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Amanda Taylor is an 72-year-old woman who presents for a follow-up for hypertension. History includes significant alcohol use disorder and mild hypercholesterolemia. At last visit on April 16, BP was 130/90. After presenting at 150/100 she was prescribed hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth once daily and lisinopril 20 mg by mouth once daily. CMP and CBC were essentially unremarkable.

Mrs. Taylor admits to consuming alcohol 4-5 times a week and is interested in Naltrexone injections. She acknowledges that her blood pressure would likely improve if she stopped alcohol consumption. She is checking her blood pressure in the morning and evening, stating it is higher at night but will improve after sitting for a few minutes. She is averaged 135-145 or 150 over 95-110.

She experienced side effects from lisinopril, such as headaches, “swooshing” in her left ear, and lethargy. She took half a dose and did not experience the side effects.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Hypertension.
Hypercholesterolemia.
Alcohol use disorder.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Lisinopril 20 mg tablet once daily.
Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg once daily.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Constitutional
No distress. No fever. No tobacco or other substance use.

Neurological
Alert.

Psychological
She does not appear anxious, agitated or depressed.

Neck
JVP flat with a head at 90 degrees.

Respiratory
No cough or shortness of breath. Chest clear to percussion and auscultation. Unlabored breathing.

Cardiovascular
No CVA tenderness. Cardiac rhythm regular. No murmur. No gallop.

Musculoskeletal
No ankle edema.

Integumentary
Skin is moist. Good trigger.

ASSESSMENT

• Alcohol use disorder.
• Hypertension.

Mrs. Taylor presents for a follow-up for hypertension. History includes significant alcohol use disorder and mild hypercholesterolemia. At last visit on April 16, BP was 130/90. After presenting at 150/100 she was prescribed hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth once daily and lisinopril 20 mg by mouth once daily. CMP and CBC were essentially unremarkable.

PLAN

Alcohol use disorder
I will start her on naltrexone which we have previously discussed. She will take the injectable form and start on May 17. We reviewed side effects and risks. The potential benefit of alcohol cessation would be highly beneficial to the patient.

Hypertension
She will continue taking lisinopril 10 mg/day and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily. Quite possibly her blood pressure will improve significantly if she is able to completely stop consuming alcohol. Target BP is 130-140/70-80. I will see her back week of June 14 which likely will be the time of her second naltrexone injection.

Recommend upgrading influenza vaccination and Shingrix #2, she defers this.

INSTRUCTIONS

Call the clinic with any questions. Make follow up appointment week of June 14th.

sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Left hand pain and difficulty controlling alcohol consumption.
History of Present Illness
The patient reports experiencing pain in his left hand for several weeks, exacerbated by activity. He describes the pain as sharp and intermittent. The patient also reports struggling with not drinking and experiencing urges to drink. He reports experiencing headaches and tiredness after starting lisinopril. He reports experiencing swooshing sounds in his left ear after the first time taking lisinopril. The patient reports feeling lethargic and tired after the first time taking lisinopril. Findings
The patient's morning blood pressure readings are between 130 and 145 or 150 mmHg, and the evening readings are between 95 and 100 mmHg. The patient reports blood pressure readings of 150 mmHg in the evening. The patient reports blood pressure readings between 95 and 100 mmHg in the morning. The patient reports experiencing dizziness. sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: The doctor will administer a shot to the patient. The doctor recommends upgrading influenza vaccination. The doctor recommends shingrix. The doctor plans to discuss medicine to help with alcohol urges. Vivitrol may be administered as an alternative to naltrexone. Naltrexone will be started on May 17th. The patient will have a follow-up in June. Lisinopril will be started. Hydrochlorothiazide will be started.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient's morning blood pressure readings are between 130 and 145 or 150 mmHg, and the evening readings are between 95 and 100 mmHg.
section_miscategorizations.reason: These are home blood pressure readings reported by the patient rather than measurements obtained in clinic; they are patient-reported history rather than objective exam/vital findings.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports blood pressure readings of 150 mmHg in the evening.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a patient-reported home blood pressure value and fits better as historical symptom/monitoring data rather than objective findings.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports blood pressure readings between 95 and 100 mmHg in the morning.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Again, this is patient-reported home monitoring information, which belongs in the history rather than the objective findings section.
Dizziness is a subjective symptom reported by the patient, not an observed exam finding; placing it under Findings could mislead a reader into thinking it was objectively observed.
Anxiety here is described as a subjective experience, not a documented mental status exam finding; it belongs in the symptom history rather than objective findings.
Tiredness/fatigue is a subjective symptom; listing it as a finding suggests it was objectively observed, which could distort interpretation.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports experiencing swooshing sounds in their left ear after the first time taking lisinopril.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a subjective auditory symptom temporally related to medication use; it is part of the history of present illness rather than an objective exam or test finding.

Elsewhere in the note, lisinopril is already described as having been started with associated side effects; repeating 'will be started' in the Plan blurs the timeline. As written, it more accurately reflects past/current medication state and would fit better in HPI if clarifying when it was initiated.

Without an explicit hypertension diagnosis in the Assessment, initiating hydrochlorothiazide in the Plan lacks a clearly documented problem linkage. Ideally, the related condition (e.g., hypertension) should be added to the Assessment so the medication change is clearly tied to an active problem.
